prompt:
We got bitten badly by the units package last month: it's one process-wide table (package-level maps, an init() that preloads the standard units, a global precision setting), and when the importer started calling units.SetPrecision(6) for its own output, every dashboard number rendered by the SAME process grew six decimals. Two subsystems, one hidden global — classic. The fix we agreed on in review: make it instance-based, functional options, no package-level mutable state at all.

Target API, same package: New(opts ...Option) (*Registry, error). Options: WithStandardUnits() preloads exactly the table and aliases init() used to install; WithUnit(name, dim, factor), WithAlias(alias, canonical), WithPrecision(p) do what their global counterparts did, and a broken option (unknown canonical, duplicate name, factor <= 0, precision out of range) makes New fail with the same error text the old setters produced. Options apply in order, so a later WithPrecision overrides an earlier one. A bare New() is an EMPTY registry — the standard table is opt-in now. Registries stay mutable at runtime through methods Register, RegisterAlias, SetPrecision, plus Convert, all with the exact semantics and error strings of today's package functions ('unknown unit: X', 'dimension mismatch: kg is mass, m is length', 'unit already registered: X', 'precision out of range: N', default precision 4, round-half-away rounding to the configured decimals). Two registries must be fully independent — units, aliases, dup detection, precision, all of it. Delete the global table, the init(), and the package-level Register/RegisterAlias/SetPrecision/Convert once the instance API is in.

Conversions must come out bit-identical to the old table for the same inputs — the ingest goldens depend on it. registry_test.go is the acceptance gate: go test -race -timeout 30s ./... — and the test file stays as-is.
